interactions.summary: Jiang uses a classroom image and a school-day analogy to define ritual as the everyday expression of religion: ordered action backed by a belief system.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang with partial student participation; some student speech is noisy.

interactions.summary: Jiang reads and explains the shaman's sacred task: entering the realm of the He Spirit to restore creation when plants, animals, or the visible world are failing.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang reading quoted material with interpretive explanation.

interactions.summary: Jiang extends the argument into animism and hunting: nature and culture are not separate, hunting requires permission, and reciprocity makes meat a gift rather than a right.
Jiang reads the hunting ritual as a dramatic collapse of time and form in which the shaman moves among fish, animal, human, pure energy, myth, and ordinary reality.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang reading quoted material and glossing it.

speaker_notes.note: The early part of the segment includes noisy classroom back-and-forth; Jiang's definition of ritual is clear after the exchange.
The shaman and hunting passages include quoted anthropological material embedded in Jiang's lecture.

claims.claim: Jiang defines ritual as the expression of religion in everyday practice, where bodies, words, timing, and posture carry religious meaning.
He uses school routines as an analogy for ritual: ordered actions only make sense because an underlying belief system gives them purpose.
The shaman's authority comes from entering the spirit world, communicating with gods and demons, and restoring the energy of creation.
Jiang says this religion makes the visible world insignificant relative to the spirit world, because the spirit world controls what happens here.
In the animist model Jiang develops, nature and culture are not separate; humans, plants, animals, rivers, and forests are responsible for the collective well-being of creation.
Hunting is not a trivial act but a negotiated contract: the shaman seeks permission from animal masters or spirit guardians, and reciprocity governs the exchange.
Jiang interprets cave paintings as tribute and apology to animals whose bodies nourish humans and whose souls might otherwise seek vengeance.
The hunting ritual is presented as a sophisticated transformation in which the shaman crosses animal, human, temporal, and mythic dimensions.
claims.temporal_scope: Interpretive claim from quoted material in this lecture.

signature_moments.moment: A school schedule becomes a secular ritual, backed by belief in what school is supposed to make students become.
signature_moments.source_phrase: This is all what we call ritual
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The classroom analogy makes ritual legible without reducing it to exotic behavior.

signature_moments.moment: The shaman is compared to an engineer entering a nuclear reactor to renew the cosmic order.
signature_moments.source_phrase: like a modern engineer who enters the depths of a nuclear reactor
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It converts shamanic practice from superstition into dangerous technical maintenance of reality.

signature_moments.moment: The visible world does not matter most; the spirit world controls everything.
signature_moments.source_phrase: our world doesn't matter... What matters is the spirit world
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the strongest reversal in the lecture's ontology.

signature_moments.moment: Meat is not a hunter's right; it is a gift granted by the spirit world.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Meat is not the right of a hunter but a gift from the spirit world
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It condenses the lecture's ethics of reciprocity into one memorable reversal.

signature_moments.moment: The shaman changes from fish to animal to human and becomes pure energy across dimensions of reality.
signature_moments.source_phrase: becoming pure energy flowing among every dimension of reality
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It preserves the lecture's most vivid ritual image in this packet.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: The expression of religion in everyday practice: ordered action with meaning and purpose supplied by a belief system.
The larger order of creation that the shaman restores or renews by entering the spirit world.
Jiang glosses it as give-and-take; in hunting, it means permission, offering, and exchange with animal spirits or guardians.
A guardian or master of animals whose permission is needed for hunting and whose vengeance may follow unauthorized killing.
A quoted comparison in which hunting seeks the blessing of a greater authority for taking a precious being into one's family.
The ritual movement across fish, animal, human, energy, time, myth, and ordinary reality in order to communicate with the spirit world.

uncertainty_notes.note: The opening classroom exchange is noisy and should not be mined for exact student claims.
This segment contains heavy ASR noise in the quoted prose; the semantic claim keeps only the stable meaning Jiang states afterward: everything is connected and equal.
The transcript likely renders 'chants' as 'chance' in the hunting ritual passage; avoid exact quotation of that word without source checking.
